The production bible reintroduces non-native greenery in ways that cut against the brief’s own realism guardrails. The library scene uses “a planter edge softly present” and frames a “small trio” as the community beat, the buffet scene is literally “the table tucked by greenery,” the atrium scene adds “hanging greenery,” and the pool scene adds “palm planters.” Those are exactly the kinds of cues that make the concept feel staged or resort-generic instead of ship-native and subtly encoded. See WORK.txt:588, WORK.txt:590, WORK.txt:623, WORK.txt:660, WORK.txt:672.

The scene library drifts away from the tight one-or-two-person social grammar that makes this concept distinctive. The brief says one or two people max and “mostly solos and pairs,” but the bible expands into a “small trio,” “a small group smiling and pointing,” and “friends sink into a shaded cabana.” That weakens the low-pressure recognition system and starts to read like general vacation marketing with a soft-green veneer. See WORK.txt:102, WORK.txt:543, WORK.txt:590, WORK.txt:650, WORK.txt:674.

The image-prompt language is still too uniformly “luxury travel editorial” and stylized, which flattens differentiation. The repeated “dreamy travel editorial, aspirational, warm cinematic color grade, f/1.8 bokeh” phrasing shows up across the stills and scenes, and it pushes the output toward a generic premium-travel finish instead of a more ownable Jewel-of-the-Seas-specific visual system. Representative examples are at WORK.txt:474, WORK.txt:539, WORK.txt:566, WORK.txt:662.

A few moments still lean too poetic or synthetic rather than plausibly observed. “Leaf-shaped shadow dances across their arms,” “leaf-shadow drifts over a linen shoulder,” and “leaflike reflection skimming the glass” are elegant, but in aggregate they risk feeling generated instead of captured. One or two of these cues work; this many starts to read like visual motif enforcement rather than vacation reality. See WORK.txt:471, WORK.txt:536, WORK.txt:635.

The countdown/CTA layer is improved but still slightly generic in its closing move. “Sunset like this is three cabins away” is fine, but it sells scarcity on a generic beautiful-cruise image rather than on the campaign’s distinct social promise. That means the conversion layer is not fully cashing in the niche advantage the rest of the brief establishes. See WORK.txt:906, WORK.txt:936.

Overall, this is strong. The discovery/blueprint is not the problem. The core identity is coherent, the slogan is good, the opt-in social rules are disciplined, and the main cue family is stable. The remaining failure is still in the aesthetic/bible translation layer: it keeps relaxing into premium-cruise editorial language, greenery-adjacent staging, and broader group vacation scenes when this concept wins by being quieter, tighter, and more encoded.

Grade: A-
Recommendation: revise_then_continue

If you want the next pass to land at A/A+, I’d focus on three edits only:

Remove all explicit planter/greenery infrastructure from the bible unless it is unquestionably ship-native and background-only.
Force every hero scene back to solos or pairs, with one small family duo allowed but no trios/cabana-friend-group/tender-group beats.
Replace the generic editorial prompt suffix with a more ship-specific realism spec tied to Jewel spaces, lensing, and observational travel photography rather than “aspirational cinematic bokeh” boilerplate.
